input_semantic_node: Fetal Personhood and Moral Equality from Conception
central_semantic_node: Axiomatic Fetal Personhood as a Basis for Absolute Moral Rights
argument_reasons: Ontological continuity: The argument suggests that because a zygote contains the genetic blueprint of a human, it is essentially identical to an adult human.
Potentiality as Actuality: It treats the potential to become a person as the current possession of personhood rights.
Absence of a Non-Arbitrary Boundary: Since any point of development after conception is seen as a 'slope', conception is used as the only binary switch for personhood.

ontological_signature.function: To establish a legal and ethical prohibition by equating developmental stages with a fixed moral status.
ontological_signature.form: Linear Causal Chain (Conception → Personhood → Absolute Rights)
ontological_signature.tension: Potentiality vs. Actuality and the Conflict of Rights (Fetus vs. Gestator).
ontological_signature.limit: Depends entirely on the definition of 'person', which is a social/legal construct, not a biological one.

conceptual_disintegration.definition: A developing human lifeform inside an egg or a body.
A member of the community who has special rules to protect them and who can have their own needs.
The exact moment when a new set of instructions (DNA) is combined to start building a life.
The 'do not touch' signs we put on people to make sure they are safe and treated fairly.

preconceptual_transduction: If you have all the bricks to build a house, is it already a house? Some people say yes, because the plan is there. Others say no, because you can't live in it yet. If we say the pile of bricks is a finished house, we have to treat it with the same rules as a house where people already live.

iterations.base_statement: A fetus is a person from conception
iterations.context: Is biological life (genetic uniqueness) sufficient for the social/moral category of 'Person'?

global_evaluation.criteria: The argument relies on an axiomatic definition of 'person' that is not empirically verifiable through first principles alone, creating a persistent logical loop between biology and meta-ethics.

deductive_observations.origin: If a fetus is a person from conception AND persons have a right to life.
deductive_observations.conclusion: The fetus has a right to life from conception.
deductive_observations.notes: Direct proof if premises are accepted.

subjectivities: The definition of 'Personhood' (religious vs. secular vs. biological).
The weight given to potentiality versus actualized cognitive capacity.

counterexamples.refuted_statement: A fetus is a person from conception.
counterexamples.description: Monozygotic twinning: A single zygote can become two individuals days after conception; if personhood was fixed at conception, the 'person' would be divided or undefined.
counterexamples.notes: Questions the 'singular person' identity at the moment of conception.

counterexamples.refuted_statement: Fetal rights are equal to adult rights.
counterexamples.description: The Violinist Argument (Thomson): Even if the fetus is a person, its right to life does not automatically include the right to use another person's body against their will.
counterexamples.notes: Separates personhood from the right to bodily usage.

inductive_observations.observed_pattern: Most legal systems distinguish between the death of a fetus and the murder of an adult.
inductive_observations.inference: Societies inductively treat personhood as a graduated scale rather than a binary switch.

preconceptual_conclusion: Just because a seed will become a tree doesn't mean it is already a forest, but it still has the magic to grow into one.
emerging_theory_or_intuition: Personhood is not an 'inherent' property like mass, but a 'relational' property granted by a moral community based on the emergence of consciousness and social interaction.

rows: A fetus is a person from conception
Self-referential: The definition of person is selected specifically to include the fetus at conception, making it a tautology rather than a discovery.
A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
Undefined: Moral rights depend on the framework (Deontological vs. Utilitarian) which are not reconciled here.
False: Refuted by counter-cases where the mother's right to life is threatened, proving rights are not absolute but hierarchical.

formula_dictionary.A: A fetus is a person from conception
formula_dictionary.B: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult

why_the_formula_structure_is_incorrect: The formula assumes B (absolute rights) is a necessary consequence of A (personhood), ignoring the 'Right to Bodily Autonomy' which is a separate P value. Also, A is undefined.
boolean_formula_to_natural_language: If a fetus is a person (A) and possesses equal moral rights (B), and there is no conflicting right to bodily autonomy (D), then abortion is not justifiable (!C).
conclusion: If we define personhood purely by genetic uniqueness then the fetus is a person, however if rights are competitive and depend on consciousness as per neurological consensus (Giubilini & Minerva, 2012), then the claim of 'adult equality' fails and for all this the statement is an axiomatic anchor rather than an empirical truth. In a single line.

collapse_implications.statement: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult
collapse_implications.implication_by_false_state: If a fetus does not have equal rights, abortion becomes a matter of weighing interests/harms.
collapse_implications.implication_by_true_state: If rights are equal, gestation becomes a mandatory form of 'duty of care' similar to parental support.

logical_tension.paradox: The Potentiality Paradox: A thing cannot be identical to its future state while existing in its current state, yet we grant it the value of its future state to protect its current existence.
logical_tension.ambiguity: The term 'human' is biological; the term 'person' is moral/legal. The argument treats them as interchangeable synonyms.
logical_tension.useful_contradiction: A fetus is both 'part of' the mother's body and 'a distinct entity' simultaneously; this biological dependency creates a unique moral category that standard 'personhood' logic fails to capture.

analogous_reorganization: A blueprint for a skyscraper vs. a completed building.
A seed vs. an oak tree.
An unexecuted computer program versus a running process.
implications: If personhood is binary at conception, medical procedures like IVF (where embryos are discarded) become mass-casualty events.
If personhood is graduated, rights increase with the development of the nervous system.

global_reevaluation.criteria: The argument's truth depends entirely on the circular definition of personhood established in the premise.

reconclusion: The proposition is a metaphysical axiom that functions as a moral 'hard-stop' rather than a logical derivation; because it fails to account for the competitive nature of rights and the biological reality of dependency, it remains an incomplete system for evaluating moral justification.
preconceptual_reconclusion: You can't say a drawing of a person is the same as a person who can talk and feel, even if the drawing is the first step to making the person.
context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: A fetus has the same moral rights as an adult (context: A fetus is a person from conception (context: Is abortion morally justifiable?))))
